one_line_takeaway: By clustering per-sample explainable local feature contributions (active paths) in ReLU feed-forward networks, the authors detect and surgically remove data-poisoning backdoors in a Netflow-based intrusion detection system without retraining, restoring malicious-class detection accuracy from as low as 0.00%/5.19% under poison to ~90-91% while leaving clean-data accuracy essentially unchanged.
key_finding: Experiment 1 (single trigger feature, TTL_max=66): backdoored model achieved 99.38% clean accuracy and 99.86% poison accuracy (i.e., attack success). Table II: before elimination, All-data clean/poisoned accuracy = 99.29%/97.79%, Malicious-class accuracy collapsed to 5.19% under poison. After eliminating backdoor weights: All-data clean/poisoned = 99.30%/98.72%, Malicious-class accuracy recovered to 90.91% (poisoned) vs. 91.34% (clean, unbackdoored baseline) -- i.e., near-full recovery with negligible loss on clean data. Experiment 2 (two trigger features, TTL_max=66 & TTL_min=61): backdoored model reached 99.23% clean accuracy and 99.98% poison accuracy; Malicious-class accuracy dropped to 0.00% under poison before elimination. After elimination: All-data clean/poisoned = 99.51%/99.74%, Malicious accuracy recovered to 90.48% (poisoned) vs. 90.91% clean baseline -- again near-complete elimination with minimal degradation. Table I/III (frequency tables) empirically confirm the trigger values (TTL_max=66, TTL_min=61) are used almost exclusively by the flagged (backdoor) cluster and essentially never by the benign cluster, validating the cluster-comparison detection step. The paper notes qualitatively that contribution differences (Fig. 6 vs. Fig. 8) shrink when going from one to two backdoor features, suggesting the method may be less robust as the number of simultaneously-poisoned trigger features grows -- flagged explicitly as a possible limitation/artifact of this single synthetic dataset.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (stated as C1-C3) are: (C1) a novel, explainable backdoor detection method for feed-forward neural networks with piecewise-linear (ReLU/Leaky ReLU) activations, based on clustering per-sample local feature contributions ("active paths") derived from explainable slope coefficients (Hoyheim et al. 2025, cited as [12], which the paper explicitly builds on); (C2) a backdoor elimination method that removes the specific weights (input-to-first-hidden-layer) associated with the identified backdoor-trigger active paths, without retraining the model or relabeling data; and (C3) an application/evaluation of both methods on a network intrusion detection system (NIDS) trained on Netflow data, showing detection and near-complete elimination of an injected backdoor with minimal degradation to normal-traffic performance. Detection pipeline: (i) pass data through the trained network to get per-sample local feature contributions phi_ij = beta_ij * x_ij; (ii) reduce dimensionality with Kernel PCA (cosine kernel) then cluster with HDBSCAN; (iii) compare mean feature contributions across clusters (Algorithm 1) to flag features with abnormally large differences from the largest (assumed-benign) cluster, which a human analyst then inspects. Elimination: compare active paths (Algorithm 2/3, Appendix) used by backdoored vs. clean data above a usage-count threshold T, then zero out input-to-first-hidden-layer weights unique to the backdoor cluster/feature.
threat_model: Feed-forward NN backdoors implanted via data poisoning during training (not weight/parameter manipulation), corrupted-label (not clean-label) triggers, triggered at inference. The defender has white-box access to the trained model plus a dataset in which the trigger is sufficiently present (the paper found 1% poisoning rate sufficient in their experiments) -- it does NOT require a known-clean reference dataset, which the authors contrast favorably with methods like Neural Cleanse [26], Wang et al. [31], and Wu & Wang [30] that do require clean data. Concretely instantiated as an IDS attack: an adversary poisons a public/third-party training dataset (e.g., uploaded to Zenodo/Kaggle, or via infiltrating/bribing a data-annotation service) so that flows containing a trigger value (a specific TTL_max, and in Experiment 2 also TTL_min) are mislabeled benign, causing the trained NIDS to whitelist malicious traffic carrying that trigger. Method is restricted to piecewise-linear activation functions (ReLU used in experiments); extension to convolutional architectures is noted as plausible but untested.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) both the backdoor insertion and model training were performed by the same team -- no external/independent backdoor implantation was tested, which the authors say would better reflect real-world conditions; (2) no comparisons with other backdoor detection/removal methods were conducted on the same dataset (explicitly future work); (3) findings are based on a single synthetic dataset (AIT-IDSv2 with injected triggers), and the apparent robustness drop with 2 trigger features vs. 1 may be a dataset artifact; (4) the technique requires access to data where the trigger is present in sufficient quantity, which may not hold for public pretrained models (e.g., HuggingFace) or federated-learning settings; (5) the method cannot distinguish genuine backdoors from strong overfitting or legitimate feature correlations -- requires a human analyst with domain expertise to adjudicate; (6) restricted to piecewise-linear activation functions (ReLU/Leaky ReLU) with identifiable, distinct active paths; extension to CNNs is only speculated, not demonstrated; (7) poisoning-rate sufficiency (1% worked here) is noted to likely vary with data distribution and model architecture, citing Wang et al. [27].

datasets_used: AIT-IDSv2 Netflow dataset (Soro et al. 2022 [23]; Landauer et al. [15],[16]) -- modified by the authors to inject backdoors; 1,919,881 total Netflows (60,360 malicious, 1,874,880 benign), 80/20 development/test split with 20% of the development set held out for validation
quantitative_results: Experiment 1 (1 trigger feature, TTL_max=66): backdoor implant clean accuracy 99.38%, poison accuracy 99.86%
Experiment 1 Table II before elimination: All-data clean 99.29% / poisoned 97.79%; Benign clean 99.48% / poisoned 99.98%; Malicious clean 91.34% / poisoned 5.19%
Experiment 1 Table II after elimination: All-data clean 99.30% / poisoned 98.72%; Benign clean 99.50% / poisoned 98.90%; Malicious clean 90.91% / poisoned 90.91%
Experiment 2 (2 trigger features, TTL_max=66, TTL_min=61): backdoor implant clean accuracy 99.23%, poison accuracy 99.98%
Experiment 2 Table II before elimination: All-data clean 99.37% / poisoned 97.68%; Benign clean 99.57% / poisoned 99.99%; Malicious clean 90.91% / poisoned 0.00%
Experiment 2 Table II after elimination: All-data clean 99.51% / poisoned 99.74%; Benign clean 99.71% / poisoned 99.96%; Malicious clean 90.91% / poisoned 90.48%
Backdoor injected into 1% of training data, equally distributed among malicious and benign traffic
Model: 121 input features, 3 hidden layers, ~10,500 trainable weights, trained 20 epochs with Adam optimizer and early stopping (patience 5)
Active-path usage threshold T=50 used in elimination algorithm (Algorithm 3)
baselines_compared: No empirical baseline comparison was run in this paper. Related methods discussed only qualitatively/conceptually, not benchmarked on the same dataset: Activation Clustering (Chen et al. 2018, [4]) -- described as only using final-layer activations, losing feature-level explanation, requiring retraining for elimination; BadActs (Yi et al. 2024, [33]) -- described as detecting anomalies via a Gaussian-distribution assumption on activation space, lacking explainability, and requiring retraining unlike this paper's method; Neural Cleanse (Wang et al. 2019, [26]), Xu et al. meta neural analysis [31], and Wu & Wang adversarial neuron pruning [30] -- described as requiring a clean reference dataset, which this paper's method does not need. The authors explicitly flag lack of head-to-head comparison with these methods on the same dataset as future work.
